data.title: Lumentum Holdings: A Rising Star In Optical Technology Worth Your Investment
data.description:
Lumentum Holdings (NASDAQ:LITE), a market-leading designer and manufacturer of optical and photonic products that enable high-speed communications and advanced laser applications worldwide, has had a solid year so far, with a year-to-date increase of 92.28%.

Most of that outperformance has come over the last six months, driven by robust demand for its optical products from the proliferation of artificial intelligence ("AI") and cloud computing.

Training and using AI models in production environments (especially large language models) and cloud computing require massive, high-speed, low-latency data transfers for many applications. Since fiber optics provides significantly higher speeds and lower latency than copper cabling for data transmission, it has become the preferred technology for transferring data in AI infrastructure and modern data centers.

Lumentum manufactures the three most popular types of lasers used in fiber optic networks: Directly Modulated Lasers (DMLs), Electro-Absorption-Modulated Lasers (EMLs), and Vertical-Cavity Surface-Emitting Lasers (VCSELs). You may hear these lasers discussed in company communications.

Network engineers primarily use VCSELs for short-distance, high-bandwidth multimode (multiple light paths) fiber links, such as high-bandwidth links from servers to top-of-rack (ToR) switches, connecting different components within the same equipment rack, and up to 100-meter interconnects between switches located in different racks.

Lower-speed, shorter-distance (up to 10 km) applications use DML. Network engineers utilize DML within a data center for cost-effective, low-power, short- to medium-distance optical interconnections between servers, switches, and storage systems to transfer massive amounts of data. DML is ideal for applications like high-performance computing (HPC) clusters and storage area networks (SANs), which require low-latency links over short distances.

Higher-speed, long-distance applications, such as fiber optics between data centers, that require a "cleaner" and more stable signal use EMLs.

The company also manufactures other products beyond lasers, including coherent transceivers and ROADM (Reconfigurable Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers) Modules, which enhance data transfer efficiency, flexibility, and scalability for telecom, metro, long-haul, and submarine (undersea) networks. The company introduced its coherent transceivers to the market around 2021 and its initial ROADM products around 2016. I won't delve too deeply into the specifics of what these products do in this article. I will perhaps discuss them in future articles. Currently, it's sufficient to know that these products have helped the company to achieve an exceptional financial performance over the last six months.

More recently, the company has released its R64 Optical Circuit Switches ("OCS"), a product management expects will be a future revenue growth driver. Network engineers use OCS to establish high-speed, light-based connections between different fibers by reflecting light beams, often using tiny mirrors, without converting the signal to electrical form.

Speaking of revenue, in its latest quarter, Lumentum reported fourth-quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 revenue of $480.7 million, above the high end of its revised guidance range $440 million to $470 million. The areas investors are most interested in, specifically the Cloud & Networking Segment, drove most of its total revenue growth, with the segment's fourth-quarter revenue increasing 16% sequentially and 67% year-over-year, led by hyperscale cloud customer demand. Due to the strength of these results, management has issued total revenue guidance for the first quarter of FY 2026 of $510 million to $540 million. If the company meets the midpoint of its first-quarter guidance, it would set a new all-time quarterly revenue record.

The best part is that the company's revenue is translating into improved margins and a growing bottom line. Its fourth quarter FY 2025 non-GAAP (generally accepted accounting principles) operating margin increased 420 basis points ("bps") sequentially and over 2,000 bps year-over-year, primarily due to improved Cloud & Networking profitability.

You may notice that the company still has negative GAAP operating margins.

The main reason for its negative GAAP operating margins is due to several acquisitions, including IPG Photonics' (IPGP) telecom transmission business, and NeoPhotonics in August 2022, as well as Cloud Light Technology in November 2023. Although the bulk of one-time restructuring and integration costs are in the past, the company may still recognize some integration-related expenses, plant closures, or severance costs under GAAP. Lumentum still incurs Amortization of Intangible Assets from those acquisitions, totaling $34.6 million in the fourth quarter. When you add acquisition-related costs to the company's stock-based compensation of $40 million, the company's GAAP operating loss is $8.4 million.

However, the company's core profitability (non-GAAP operating profits) has grown rapidly over the last year. Additionally, the market anticipates GAAP profitability soon, given the company's positive non-GAAP operating margin guidance.

The company has forecasted a non-GAAP first quarter FY 2026 operating margin of 16–17.5%. This upward trend in non-GAAP metrics from 15% in the fourth quarter of FY2025 suggests that GAAP profitability is likely in the near future. However, since the market has likely already factored in future GAAP operating margins, don't expect GAAP profitability to be a catalyst for further upside.

The company reported non-GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") of $1.52, which exceeded the high end of the company's guidance.

Since some may consider the stock overvalued based on some valuation metrics, is it still worthwhile to invest in it today? The answer is yes for aggressive growth investors. The company continues to innovate, and management has planned to begin sampling a product that is expected to have a significant impact on Lumentum's revenue and market position soon. Therefore, the company has a solid catalyst for future upside ahead.

A next-generation approach

One product I would like to discuss is Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) with ultra-high-power lasers. CPO is the next-generation approach to enhancing computing performance by integrating VCSELs into a separate optical engine that is then co-packaged with a switch ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) on the same substrate. This arrangement significantly reduces the electrical path between the laser and switch ASIC compared to traditional pluggable optics, making the switch more energy-efficient.

Lumentum is developing CPO in collaboration with NVIDIA (NVDA), a partnership that is central to NVIDIA's effort to develop energy-efficient network switches for "AI factories." A Lumentum March 18, 2025 press release stated: "Scaling AI factories presents significant power consumption challenges. NVIDIA's newly announced Spectrum-X Photonics and Quantum-X Photonics networking switches integrate photonic innovations, including Lumentum's high-power, high-efficiency lasers, to significantly reduce power consumption, enhance resilience, and enable faster deployment." Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Michael E. Hurlston said on the fourth quarter earnings call that he expects "a significant revenue ramp in CPO by the second half of calendar 2026." He also stated:

Today, our components business is a key foundation of our success, and we are well positioned for significant growth in our cloud modules business and to lead in emerging technologies like OCS and CPO. As we scale production, expand capacity and intensify our focus on high-growth, high-margin opportunities, we are confident in our ability to deliver continued top line growth, margin expansion and long-term shareholder value.

If OCS and CPO pan out for the company, the stock price may still increase from here, despite some valuation measures flashing red.

Valuation

Lumentum Holdings' trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 6.797, which is well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, suggesting overvaluation or that the market expects future revenue growth, or that the quality of revenue will improve (increased margins). If the stock were to trade at its five-year median, the price would be $81.70, representing a 48% decline from its October 7 closing price of $157.15.

The following table compares Lumentum's 2026 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) and TTM profitability metrics to several of its peers in the laser industry.

Company Name 2026 Forward P/S ratio 2026 Forward estimated revenue growth 2026 Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Lumentum Holdings 6.10 39.53% 0.15 -6.36% 23.89% -10.97% 0.73%
Coherent (COHR) 3.19 7.92% 0.40 3.31% 36.08% 10.42% 0.25%
IPG Photonics 3.69 7.27% 0.51 6.83% 33.81% -0.71% -10.12%%%
Viavi Solutions (VIAV) 2.38 10.00% 0.24 6.20% 56.84% 5.38% 2.48%
Marvell (MRVL) 9.14 40.95% 0.22 21.14% 44.64% 6.02% -0.57%
Ciena (CIEN) 3.95 16.98% 0.23 13.33% 38.76% 5.60% 3.19%
Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) 2.39 55.88% 0.04 -66.37% 28.72% -13.02% -35.64%

Typically, the market considers a company with a lower P/S/G ratio to be undervalued compared to companies with higher P/S/G ratios. Following that rationale, the market undervalues Lumentum relative to its peers, except Applied Optoelectronics. However, when comparing some of its profitability metrics, Lumentum may deserve its lower valuation compared to most of its peers. For instance, Lumentum has the second-lowest FCF margin at -6.36%. Except for Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum is also the only company with negative FCF.

Lumentum also has the lowest TTM gross margin and the second-lowest TTM operating margin. Considering all these factors, it may deserve its lower valuation, and some investors may be unwilling to award it a premium valuation compared to its peers because of its inferior FCF and profitability.

The following chart illustrates Lumentum's EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) growth, increasing by 24.19% from $593.94 million to $916.03 million.

The following table compares Lumentum's EV/EBITDA, two-year EBITDA CAGR, and TTM EBITDA margin using metrics derived from YCharts. The market factors in the EBITDA growth rate, EBITDA margin, potential sustainability of EBITDA growth, and quality of the balance sheet. Sometimes these other factors are why companies with higher EBITDA growth rates or EBITDA margins don't always have a higher EV/EBITDA valuation.

Company name EV/EBITDA Two-year EBITDA CAGR TTM EBITDA margin
Applied Optoelectronics 63.56 111.49% -7.13
Ciena 35.28 33.73% 8.61%
Marvell 25.28 14.92% 24.33%
IPG 24.63 43.47% 5.75%
Lumentum Holdings 21.82 24.19% 4.47%
Coherent 16.01 16.25% 19.65%
Viavi 13.47 11.72% 11.16%

One significant issue that Lumentum has, which may be a reason investors discount its valuation in this comparison and in all other valuation metrics, is the state of its balance sheet. At the end of its fiscal fourth quarter, the company had $877.10 million in cash and short-term investments, compared to $2.608 billion in long-term debt.

Lumentum's debt-to-equity ratio (D/E) is 2.299, indicating that the company primarily funds its operations through debt rather than equity. A D/E above 2.0 may be a sign of financial risk. It also has a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 11.83. Generally, the market finds a debt-to-EBITDA ratio above 5.0 a sign of high leverage, which is a key risk for Lumentum, potentially making it difficult for the company to pay down debt if its EBITDA growth slows.

According to Cbonds, S&P Global Ratings rates Lumentum Holdings' LT credit at "B" with a negative outlook. A S&P Global rating of B is a highly speculative, non-investment-grade rating. A negative outlook means the rating is in danger of dropping to B-, which is one grade above a "substantial risks" rating.

Lumentum's GAAP TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) is 434.05, well above the Information Technology ("IT") sector median of 31.39, suggesting either overvaluation or that the market expects earnings to multiply over the next several years.

The company's non-GAAP TTM P/E was 78.34, above the IT sector median of 24.94, which some may consider a sign of the market overvaluing the stock.

The stock's 2026 forward P/E to estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.24, calculated by dividing its 2026 forward P/E of 33.06 by its estimated EPS growth of 135.71%. The market typically considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of an undervalued stock. Suppose it sold at a perceived fair valuation of 1.0; its price would be $659.55, a 320% rise from its October 7 closing price of $157.15.

However, although the company's PEG ratio implies a significant upside, the market may never value it at a 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0 due to analysts' and the market's skepticism of the company sustaining such high EPS growth rates, as evidenced by analysts' rapid estimated EPS growth deceleration from 135.71% in FY 2026 to 33.46% in FY 2027 and 12.90% in FY 2028. The market rarely rewards stocks with potential decelerating growth. More often, the market will discount stocks with decelerating growth. Additionally, the company's negative FCF, in concert with its balance sheet situation, may discourage the market from increasing its valuation much beyond where it is now, without a significant catalyst.

Risks

Lumentum relies on large, capital-intensive infrastructure buildouts from hyperscalers and telecom providers, which have historically exhibited significant cyclicality. The market may fear the potential downside from an inventory-driven recession/slowdown similar to the 2022–2023 post-pandemic inventory correction in hardware and the 2001 dot-com bubble burst. Occasionally, the market overinvests in new hardware technology, and when demand slackens, the bubble can burst quickly—a legitimate concern for investors. This dynamic may be another reason the market discounts the stock's valuation.

The company faces execution risks in bringing new products, such as 200G EMLs, OCS, and CPO, onto the market. Suppose Lumentum runs into manufacturing or yield delays when ramping these new products up to full production; the company's EPS may fail to reach analysts' estimates. Investors would likely sell off the stock in response.

Investors may also be accounting for the fiercely competitive optical components industry. The market may assume that competitive factors will introduce pricing pressure, resulting in a faster erosion of the new product's gross margins than Lumentum can counter through cost reductions, which could slow profit growth below expectations.

Another issue that investors may factor into the company's valuation is the company's lack of FCF; It burned $104.70 million in cash over the last year.

Much of the reason for its 2025 annual negative FCF is its increased annual capital expenditure ("CapEx"), TTM $231 million as of the fourth quarter of 2025. Management invested most of that CapEx in the company's Cloud segment. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Wajid Ali stated on the company's fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call, "In Q4, we invested $59 million in CapEx, primarily focused on manufacturing capacity to support cloud customers."

Although the company generated a positive FCF of $10.10 million in the fourth quarter, it still spends significant funds on CapEx. Management may still need to increase CapEx to maintain its edge in its Cloud & Networking Segment. So, the company may continue burning cash, at least in the near to medium term. As a result, some investors may continue questioning the quality of Lumentum's revenue until the company can show steady FCF growth.

The company's lack of FCF may eventually put the company in a precarious position since its balance sheet is not in the best shape. The company could quickly run into trouble if EBITDA growth slows.

While not an immediate risk, if the company ever falls into financial distress, which investors may identify by a rising debt-to-EBITDA ratio and debt rating agencies downgrading its debt to a point where default becomes a real possibility, it would be a thesis-busting event. An example would be S&P downgrading the debt to CCC.

Lumentum is a buy for aggressive growth investors

Value or risk-averse investors are better off avoiding this stock. The company's negative cash flow, GAAP unprofitability, and questionable balance sheet may harm the company if Lumentum encounters execution risks or enters a cyclical downturn.

However, if the company's new products succeed, especially its 200G EMLs, OCS, and its future CPO collaboration with NVIDIA, the company may have a long-term revenue and EPS growth runway that could entice growth investors. This stock may be suitable for aggressive growth investors who understand when to hold (when a company is experiencing temporary difficulties) and when to sell (when a truly thesis-busting event occurs). Only investors with the mental fortitude and patience to stay invested, even when the company encounters performance hiccups, should consider investing in this stock.

Although I don't expect the stock to achieve a 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0, I believe it can trade much higher than 0.24, especially if its new products succeed and it continues improving its profitability and FCF. As a result, I rate Lumentum a buy for aggressive growth investors only.

data.title: Yield Hunting Part 34: Above 7% From Horizon Technology's Baby Bond
data.description:
We continue our series on yield hunting, with above 7% from (NYSE:HTFC) - Horizon Technology Finance's (NASDAQ:HRZN) baby bond.

Company Overview

Here are the basic metrics that we need for the analysis of the company:

  • Total assets (2nd Q 2025): $726.8 million
  • Holdings: close to 99
  • Shares outstanding: 42.5 million
  • Current market capitalization: $261.66 million

The common stock of the company is currently traded at $6.01 with a 10.96% discount:

The 5-year total return on NAV is 2.14%, and the 10-year is 4.47%.

And here is the 10-year historical premium/discount chart of the company:

The total leverage is around 59%, and the non-leverage expense ratio is 6.31%. The gross asset non-leverage expense ratio is 2.68%. It has a 1-year negative z-score of -1.81, as shown in the picture below.

In 2023, Horizon was acquired by Monroe Capital, which gave Horizon access to Monroe’s platform, expertise, and resources. This allowed Horizon to expand its capabilities in sourcing agricultural partners and debt financing for fast-growing companies in the technology, biotechnology, conservation, information services, and sustainability sectors.

Baby bonds

HRZN has 2 baby bonds:

(HTFC) and (HTFB) are not rated by the credit agencies like Moody's, S&P, and Fitch. Both are currently priced below par in the market at the time of writing, and both are callable anytime - their call date has passed. We are more interested in HTFC because of its higher yield, of course.

(HTFC) is priced at $24.71, below par at the moment. Its yield to worst is 7.43%. It was on 06/15/2024, so it is callable anytime. The bond has 1.68 years to maturity, with a maturity date of 06/15/2027 and a yield to maturity of 7.43%. It pays 6.25% annual interest, or $1.5625 per annum, in the form of $0.390625 per quarter. HRZN issued 2 million shares with a $25 par value each of HTFC on 06/08/2022.

HTFC is one of the smartest choices in the sector at the moment:

When we compare it to the BDC over-the-counter universe, we can see the bond is in the top again:

And in the table below, you can see that we are ready to hold HTFC until it reaches par:

Strengths And Weaknesses

As we mentioned earlier, the company has a leverage of about 59%. This provides it with an asset coverage ratio cushion of about 13%, and the total asset coverage ratio is about 170%. Compared to other companies in the sector, it is not one of the highest rates here, but it is decent. Over the past three years, its return on assets has been around 2%. Of its entire loan portfolio, about 2.36% of loans are non-accrual, which is one of the moderate results in this indicator in the sector. Nearly 84% of its assets are secured by senior assets, and the secured debt is around 30%. In terms of its debt structure, HRZN still lags behind industry leaders such as Ares Capital (ARCC).

Conclusion

Horizon Technology Finance is an attractive option for investors seeking yield in the BDC sector, especially through the HTFC bond, which offers a 7.43% yield at a price below par.

data.title: U.S. Equities: A Constructive Setup For 2026
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

S&P 500 performance around Fed cuts in recessionary and non-recessionary environments

Median S&P 500 performance after Fed cuts rates since 1970, month 0 = first cut, rebased to 100 at month 0

Even after a six-month surge that lifted the S&P 500 more than 35% from its Liberation Day trough, equity markets appear to have further room to run. The combination of Fed rate cuts in a non-recessionary environment and continued momentum in big tech supports a constructive outlook for 2026 earnings and market performance.

AI-related capex has kept technology at the center of the rally, but strength is broadening. Cyclical sectors, including consumer, industrials, and financials, are now participating, and the Russell 2000, with less tech exposure, has even outpaced the S&P 500 since April. This broadening reflects growing confidence in the earnings outlook and suggests investors are beginning to price in a more durable expansion.

Policy remains a key tailwind. Historically, equities perform well in the two years following the start of a non-recessionary easing cycle, while returns are muted when cuts occur during recessions. Today, while labor market data has softened, broader indicators remain resilient, pointing to only modest slowing. With recession risks subdued, Fed easing will provide additional support to the economy.

Risks remain, including a sharper-than-expected downturn in the labor market or renewed fiscal stress, which pushes bond yields higher, offsetting the benefits of easier policy. But the powerful combination of Fed rate cuts, a deregulatory policy agenda, enhanced tax incentives, and sustained AI-driven investment suggests a strong setup for risk assets into 2026.

Original Post

data.title: McKesson Corporation Transforming Towards High-Margin Operations: Why I Choose To Buy
data.description:
McKesson Corporation (NYSE:MCK) is currently a company undergoing a major strategic transformation. The company is moving from its traditional pharmaceutical distributor into a diversified, tech-enabled healthcare services leader. The company is skewing towards high-margin services after successful acquisitions and now the recent restructurings announced last month. I'm believing in this new path and am hopeful it will enhance long-term shareholder value creation. Due to that, I'm assigning a BUY rating on MCK stock.

Understanding McKesson's Model

McKesson's existence is anchored on making healthcare more efficient by connecting drugmakers, pharmacies, and providers through large-scale distribution and digital health services. The company combines pharmaceutical distribution with higher-margin oncology, biopharma and technology solutions.

Its revenue is generated from drug logistics, data-driven access services, and specialty care management. McKesson's core businesses are mature, while oncology and biopharma platforms are driving growth.

McKesson is now growing its Core Ventures and PRISM Vision businesses while developing new ways to expand its specialty care network. It’s also investing in smarter, automated systems to move medicines more efficiently. Additionally, the company is creating digital tools that make it easier and cheaper for patients to get the treatments they require.

Brief Q1 FY26 Performance

In Q1 FY26, the company displayed strong diversified revenue growth from different drivers.

Total revenue stood at $97.8 billion, up 23% year over year ((YoY)). This growth was led by the U.S. Pharmaceutical segment at $90 billion (+25%). This segment's growth was driven by higher prescription volumes, new strategic accounts, and $12.1 billion in GLP-1 drug sales (+38%).

Meanwhile, Prescription Technology Solutions contributed $1.4 billion (+16%) from strong prior authorization and affordability programs. On the other hand, Medical-Surgical Solutions added $2.7 billion (+2%) through specialty product demand. The International segment, mainly dominated by Canada, delivered $3.7 billion (+1%) in revenue.

Overall, it's important to note how critical McKesson's specialty and oncology expansion through Core Ventures and PRISM Vision are contributing to the company. These services are strengthening the group's high-margin topline growth.

McKesson’s 2025 Acquisitions

The company, in April 2025, acquired PRISM Vision Holdings as a way to enter the retina and ophthalmology space. By so doing, the company was expanding its reach beyond oncology.

Additionally, in June 2025, as a way of strengthening community oncology networks, purchased Core Ventures from Florida Cancer Specialists.

I believe that both deals enhance McKesson’s specialty care capabilities, integrate practice management and data-driven services. The moves also anchor the new Oncology & Multispecialty segment for sustained high-margin growth.

Assessing McKesson's Recent Organisational Restructuring

In September 2025, McKesson shared some very important updates, unveiling a major organizational restructuring. The company also updated long-term financial targets, marking a pivotal shift toward higher-margin, technology-enabled healthcare services.

McKesson consolidated its U.S. and Canadian drug distribution operations into a single North American Pharmaceutical segment. It also created a new Oncology & Multispecialty segment to showcase its growing platforms in community oncology, retina, and specialty care (Core Ventures and PRISM Vision). Meanwhile, Prescription Technology Solutions remains focused on digital access and affordability, while Medical-Surgical Solutions is being prepared for separation into an independent company.

I believe that this structure enhances strategic clarity, aligning reporting with McKesson’s true growth engines. It also improves transparency around capital allocation.

At its Investor Day, McKesson raised FY26 earnings per share (EPS) guidance to $38.05–$38.55 and increased its long-term adjusted EPS growth target to 13–16%. To me this is a sign of confidence in durable earnings momentum.

I believe that this reorganization positions McKesson to highlight and accelerate growth in oncology, specialty distribution, and digital solutions. Most noteworthy, these are areas with higher margins and lower cyclicality than traditional distribution.

Overall, I view McKesson as a company transitioning into a more focused, data-driven healthcare enterprise with stronger long-term growth potential.

Key Risks to Lookout For

McKesson is faced with several key risks as it transitions to its new structure.

Operationally, I view the integration of recent acquisitions and the execution of the Medical-Surgical spin-off potentially creating temporary disruption or cost inefficiencies. I also think that the heavy reliance on GLP-1 medication volumes introduces potential volatility especially if payer policies tighten or competition increases.

Meanwhile, the company is also exposed to regulatory risks particularly around Medicare price negotiations and Drug Supply Chain Security Act (DSCSA) compliance. For McKesson, compliance means upgrading systems for unit-level serialization, product verification, and data exchange across its vast distribution network.

The company also faces macroeconomic challenges in terms of drug pricing pressures, geopolitical uncertainty, and potential supply chain disruptions. Additionally, technology and automation investments must deliver expected efficiencies to sustain margins. Managing these complexities while maintaining capital discipline and growth across oncology, multispecialty, and digital platforms remains McKesson’s most significant execution challenge.

Competition

McKesson operates in a more oligopolistic market. Its primary competitors are Cencora and Cardinal Health. These three companies dominate the U.S. pharmaceutical distribution.

Cencora has so far expanded patient access programs as well as global distribution partnerships, thereby deepening focus on specialty and biopharma services. Meanwhile, Cardinal Health has been emphasizing medical segment growth, digital supply chain optimization, and biosimilar distribution.

In contrast, McKesson is taking a different direction, which I also believe is a commendable thing, moving faster toward higher-margin areas namely oncology, multispecialty services, and prescription technology solutions. I believe that the company's leadership in automation, data-driven insights, and practice management platforms gives it stronger integration across care delivery. I see McKesson positioning itself ahead of peers and I foresee sustainable long-term profitability.

Cash Positioning

McKesson closed its Q1 in a strong cash position with $2.4 billion in cash and equivalents. This position was supported by consistent free cash flow generation and disciplined capital management.

A one-time cash payment of $3.4 billion spent on Core Ventures and PRISM Vision contributed to a negative free cash flow of $1.1 billion during the quarter. The company guided its full-year free cash flow to $4.4–$4.8 billion, indicating strong rebound capacity. The company had a $2 billion bond issuance in early FY26, which I think further strengthens liquidity without overleveraging.

I view McKesson’s steady operating income, predictable cash inflows from distribution, and rising profitability in oncology and digital solutions as giving it ample runway to self-fund pipeline growth and strategic investments without equity dilution. Overall, I think McKesson's cash burn is controlled and temporary and aligned with expansion.

Valuation

McKesson trades at a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 15.23, above both the industry average of 12.33 and slightly higher than key rival Cencora (14.50). I believe that this premium suggests investors value the company's stronger growth visibility, higher-margin specialty and technology platforms, and disciplined capital returns. The market likely recognizes McKesson’s progress in oncology, multispecialty, and digital solutions as structural differentiators. However, I think the modest gap over Cencora implies both companies are viewed as leaders in a mature, consolidated sector with similar fundamentals, although McKesson enjoys a slight strategic and operational edge.

Conclusion

McKesson’s strategic shift towards specialty care is driving steady and long-term growth, in my view. The company's balance sheet, cash generation, and disciplined execution give confidence in sustained double-digit EPS expansion. McKesson's trading at a modest premium to peers is justified in my view given its superior diversification and innovation momentum. Near-term integration and spin-off execution risks remain manageable within its robust fundamentals. Given its growth trajectory and operational strength, the outlook remains compelling; hence, my BUY rating.

data.title: KBWY: Focus On Small-Cap REITs Creates Vulnerability To Interest Rates
data.description:
Overview

Invesco KBW Premium Yield Equity REIT ETF (NASDAQ:KBWY) is a real estate-focused ETF that provides a concentrated exposure to small-cap and mid-cap REITs. When I previously covered KBWY, I issued a sell rating due to the flawed operating strategy and poor dividend coverage. Since the time of my last coverage, the macro environment has shifted and the ETF has released an updated report. Therefore, I wanted to revisit this ETF to reassess whether or not it is a great time to begin accumulating shares.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that KBWY's share price has declined by more than 25%. The fund has failed to participate in the market rally over the last few months. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 17.6% over the same period. KBWY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 9.7%, but I believe that this generous distribution is negatively impacting the share price.

Unfortunately, I do not believe that performance for KBWY will improve until interest rates are substantially lower. Since the fund is exclusively focused on real estate, it's understandable why performance has been so poor. The real estate sector has continued to lag behind the rest of the market, due to the higher cost of borrowing. However, there are some more attractive peers for investors that still wish to maintain exposure to the sector. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that KBWY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, KBWY has total assets under management of $246.5M that is spread across 27 different holdings. The fund aims to invest at least 90% of its assets into small-cap and mid-cap equity REITs that offer competitive dividend yields. By including a focus on small-cap REITs, KBWY has the potential to ride the positive growth of these businesses as they expand earnings and grow their base assets over time. The fund rebalances and reconstitutes its holdings on the third Friday of every March, June, September, and December.

Looking at the breakdown of its holdings by sector, we can see that KBWY maintains diversity across many sub-sectors of real estate. For instance, KBWY is most heavily allocated to Diversified REITs at 19.90% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Health Care and Office REITs, which account for 19.05% and 18.60% of assets, respectively. The combination of the top three sectors accounts for over 57% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors have the greatest impact on KBWY's daily price movement and total return potential.

KBWY takes a very concentrated approach to its holdings, since there are only 27 positions within. Therefore, I wanted to share some of the most notable traits of some of its top holdings. KBWY's top holding is Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN), which offers a high 8.2% dividend yield and has seen its share price heavily suppressed over the last few years. Fellow analyst, Luuk Wierenga, offers a very compelling argument on why BDN is currently a strong buy. Here are some of the notable callouts for some of KBWY's other holdings:

  • Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR): Recently outlined a $270M investment to shift its portfolio into the life science sector, while also targeting a 7% annual growth in the cannabis market.
  • Gladstone Commercial Corporation (GOOD): Continues to expand its portfolio and maintains a high occupancy rate.
  • Apple Hospitality REIT (APLE): Recently outlined a $80M to $90M reinvestment plan.

The unfortunate part is that many of these small-cap and mid-cap REITs are very reliant on access to cheap debt capital. If you look at the share price of these top holdings over the last few years, we can see their share prices trending downward. This is because the majority of KBWY's top holdings have been negatively impacted by the higher interest rate environment. For reference, the Fed recently initiated a 25 basis point cut to interest rates in September. However, interest rates still remain near the higher end of their range over the last decade, which has made it challenging for these REITs to grow.

A higher interest rate means that REITs do not have access to cheap debt financing. Therefore, any growth initiatives, such as new development or construction of properties may be delayed and put on backlog. If any of their debts were taken on a floating rate basis, many of the REITs will experience a rising interest expense, which causes management to shift their focus on capital efficiency more than growth. Therefore, risk is likely to remain limited until rates are substantially lower.

Performance Risk & Comparison

However, the higher interest rates aren't the only issue for KBWY at this time. KBWY's starting dividend yield sits at 9.8%, but I believe this is too generous. Most of KBWY's positions have remained in a downtrend over the last two years, and most of them do not offer dividend yields above 8%. As a result, there's no way that KBWY has been able to generate a sufficient level of income to actually support the distributions that are being paid out. For instance, the most updated report available to shareholders reveals that KBWY only generated $8.5M in total investment income, while recognizing a net realized loss of $26.4M.

The combination of the flawed operating strategy and unfavorable macro conditions has resulted in poor performance since KBWY's inception. For reference, we can see that KBWY's share price has declined by 58.6% since inception. Even when including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at a loss of 17.4% over the same period. Therefore, I would advise investors to think about the opportunity cost of holding onto a REIT ETF like KBWY, since there are more attractive opportunities available.

In an effort to provide some alternatives for investors, I wanted to highlight two other choices that can provide exposure to the real estate sector. One of the funds operates as a closed end fund that specifically puts emphasis on income generation, while the other is a more traditional ETF that provides a diversified exposure across the sector.

  • Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI)
  • Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ)

As we can see below, KBWY is the smallest fund of the bunch and has the shortest operating history. VNQ has a much lower expense ratio and $65.6B in total assets under management. I recently covered RQI and issued a buy rating because of its favorable valuation and attractive earnings history.

When running a performance comparison over the last decade, we can see that RQI has the highest total return of the bunch. RQI offers a dividend yield of 7.8% and monthly payouts, which is very competitive. Additionally, RQI includes a focus on large-cap holdings, which has helped it navigate the higher interest rate environment more efficiently. Even VNQ's approach with approximately 158 holdings leads to outperformance over the same time frame.

Dividend Isn't Sustainable

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1258 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of about $1.5096 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, we can see that the fund has failed to generate a sufficient level of earnings on a consistent basis. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($8.26) per share
  • 2021: $6.63 per share
  • 2022: ($0.49) per share
  • 2023: ($1.34) per share
  • 2024: $2.83 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): ($2.00) per share

Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that the payouts have continually trended downward over the last decade. Therefore, KBWY is not a great choice for income investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income from the real estate sector. The main reason why investors choose to maintain exposure to a high yield asset is for the income potential. If that income is always under the threat of reduction, it defeats the purpose of staying invested.

Since the distributions are typically fueled by net investment income, the payouts received from KBWY may not be the most tax-efficient. So if you are still compelled to initiate a position in KBWY, a position in would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain a sell rating on KBWY at this time due to the poor performance as interest rates remain elevated. The focus on small-cap and mid-cap REITs makes the ETF very vulnerable to higher borrowing costs. As a result, many of the top holdings within KBWY have experienced sharp declines over the last two years and I don't believe that performance will improve until rates are substantially lower. So although the Fed recently cut rates by 25 basis points, I do not believe this is sizeable enough to have a positive impact on performance.

data.title: Dorchester Minerals: Very High-Yield Oil Royalty With Zero Debt
data.description:
Introduction

Since I first covered them back in August, Dorchester Minerals, L.P. (NASDAQ:DMLP) is down a few percentage points due to continued macro uncertainty.

However, with some tailwinds advancing and high potential to see a strong recovery on top of a ~9% or even higher potential dividend, I would like to go through these developments and update the thesis, as I believe this is still an opportunity - perhaps even better now.

Recent Quarter

As we can see, the company’s net income has dropped significantly (almost 60% YoY drop per common), in part as a result of a much lower net income combined with a higher number of common units, which is ultimately how they expand.

Still, they’ve been constantly adding reserves over the years and even announced another acquisition of mineral interests in Colorado recently. This multi-basin exposure and constant acquisitions could ultimately mean that they’ll continue to do it, and given their financial position and weaker oil prices, I think it’s likely to continue to see more announcements like this over time, potentially even looking into a larger merger or acquisition if they deem it to be accretive, which is something we’ve seen in the industry even recently [see the Viper Energy (VNOM) and Sitio Royalties acquisition that I talked about recently].

Their Q3 earnings report is expected to be released on October 30 and should be quite interesting. I will pay close attention to how they view the current environment, as weaker prices could also allow them to expand significantly at better prices if they’re willing to take advantage of it. Again, as I pointed out in the initial analysis, we have to remember that DMLP is in an exceptional financial position, with no debt and the current assets covering their total liabilities more than 10 times, so getting a bit of debt to expand at a low cost could be a possible move.

Macro Developments

Rate cuts are something that I mention for most other companies as their main near-term catalyst, as they could boost the demand for oil since they’d revitalize the economy, which is ultimately the reason why the Fed uses them. With two more rate cuts expected following the one in September and potentially even more next year (maybe even more after Powell’s term ends in May 2026), we’re likely looking at a clear catalyst coming our way.

We’ve seen continued pressure on oil prices during this time, with a very important factor being OPEC, which is allegedly planning to keep production high again in November (which came with even more pressure on oil recently) after doing it for most of the previous months this year. I’ll add here a quote from TotalEnergies (TTE) Q2 earnings report, where they summarize this environment quite well, saying they see signals that:

(...) the oil market is well supplied, in particular, fueled by OPEC+ decision to unwind some voluntary production cuts and also facing a weaker demand linked to the global slowdown of economic growth

Combined with China’s easing policy advancing, together with US rate cuts and the potential for tariff uncertainty to reduce, we could be looking at an improved environment for DMLP.

However, until it improves, we remain in a risky situation. Again, gold breached $4,000 per ounce for a reason, and it’s definitely not because the economy is doing great. This is an all-time record after breaking several more records this year. If we’re looking at a hard landing/recession/etc., we could continue to see pressure for oil prices due to weaker US demand. This would eventually lead to a cut in production as well since we’re in CAPEX-cutting territory, leading to the classic cyclicality effect, but that can take years.

Another development I’d like to mention more as a precaution (not to think this news helps them directly) or a potential opportunity for the future is the growth in the market’s interest in building data centers near oilfields. This would technically come with significant energy/water/etc. infrastructure that could be dedicated to the data centers, although the logistics behind it may not seem that feasible. Still, I mention this because we've recently seen LandBridge (LB) and NRG Energy (NRG) entering into a strategic collaboration to explore the development of a potential data center site in Texas' Delaware Basin.

However, similar to what I said in the Freehold Royalties (OTCPK:FRHLF) stock analysis published recently, we have to remember that we’re looking at a different business model, with DMLP’s being centered around owning mineral rights and royalty interests - so basically what’s beneath the surface, not the surface itself. LandBridge is a landowner, which is a very important difference. Still, attracting data centers “near or above their minerals” can still be something beneficial, as it would result in more demand for their interests/titles since it would be much easier and potentially cheaper to benefit from nearby infrastructure. This could also attract DMLP into acquiring surface rights as well in the future, and if we give them half a decade at the rates they’ve been replacing their reserves at, we’re likely looking at a very different story.

Reiterated Valuation

The valuation doesn’t warrant a change in my opinion since the recent developments kind of balance each other, and oil prices will ultimately be the key factor. Again, to adjust for the lower oil prices compared to 2024’s average, I believe we can still expect DMLP to make somewhere around $80 million this year if oil prices remain at the current levels, as they don’t have debt and don’t have to worry about CAPEX. Again, I'd like to reiterate that oil prices will dictate this at the end of the day, so volatility is a given.

To adjust for the volatility, let’s assume a CAGR of only 6% over the next 5 years, followed by 4% for the subsequent 5, as the company has proven to be very willing to take advantage of a higher stock price to develop, which will usually happen during the upcycle (the past 10-year CAGR in FCF/share should be around ~10.85% based on the $80 million expected), but accounting for an overall weaker environment due to the smaller profits of new wells.

Assuming a terminal growth rate of 2% and a WACC of 8% (assumptions: 8% cost of equity, no debt, no tax rate since it’s a limited partnership), the resulting DCF model gets us to an enterprise value of ~$1.68 billion. After adding the $36.51 million in net cash, this leads to an equity value of ~$1.717 billion. With 47.34 million shares outstanding (before the recent acquisition), this gives an intrinsic value of about $36.26 per share, leaving a decent margin of safety from the current level. However, this assumes growing cash flows going forward, which is very unlikely due to oil’s volatility, as this is definitely not the bottom.

Conclusions

To reiterate, I continue to rate DMLP a buy now given their exceptional financial health and relatively low valuation that still leaves a margin of safety and a very good dividend yield. However, keep in mind that since volatility is very likely, a dividend cut would also naturally come, which could put even more pressure on the stock price. Still, I believe DMLP should remain a solid long-term investment that I am looking forward to adding especially on weaker prices.

data.title: Sabra Health Care: SHOP Model As A Value Catalyst
data.description:
Sabra Health Care REIT, Inc. (NASDAQ:SBRA) has attractive FFO prospects and positive expansion toward a higher-growth segment (SHOP), making it a good buy with a target price of $19.22.

The Company

The company provides its investments in the healthcare sector of the United States and Canada, positioning itself as a mid-sized REIT with a market cap of $4.46 billion. Its portfolio as of June 2025 consisted of 359 properties with more than 36,000 beds and units.

Its portfolio is mostly composed of Triple-Net (NNN) leases, where tenants are responsible for the operating costs of the property, offering the company predictable and stable cash flow, however, this limits the potential rent increases to annual contractual adjustments. The other portion of its portfolio is composed of the Senior Housing Operating Portfolio (SHOP), formed by senior living communities managed by third parties under a RIDEA structure, where Sabra takes direct participation in the financial performance of the properties, capturing NOI growth and offering significant growth potential. These models are the predominant structures the company uses to generate its revenues, and based on Q2 data, 79.1% of NOI comes from NNN (Skilled Nursing/Transitional Care, Behavioral Health, Senior Housing and Specialty Hospitals & Other), and 20.9% from SHOP (Senior Housing – Managed).

The transition to a larger SHOP portfolio aims to capture accelerated NOI growth, even though it introduces more volatility compared to the NNN model. This shift is already showing results, considering that SHOP represented 20.9% of NOI in Q2 of this year, and as this share increases, growth will be even stronger, since compared to the same quarter of the previous year, the percentages are already higher.

This expansion is its main growth driver, and its thesis is supported by positive evidence of both organic growth (improvement of existing properties) and inorganic growth (new acquisitions). Management’s guidance for 2025 points to an increase in the SHOP portfolio NOI between 10% and 15% on a Same-Store basis, and this growth is complemented by an aggressive inorganic expansion, where in the second quarter of 2025 the company acquired a senior housing property for $53 million, maintaining an investment pipeline of approximately $350 million to further accelerate this transition.

The organic growth mentioned in the improvement of properties refers to the increase in occupancy driven by the demand for senior housing, which has been strongly recovering after the pandemic, being demographically favorable to fill vacant beds and units. A revenue with low marginal cost is added for each of these new tenants, resulting in a strong positive impact on its operating income. Furthermore, SBRA gains pricing power as this demand heats up as the supply of new properties is limited due to high construction costs, so for this reason, its operators are able to increase monthly rental rates per resident, passing on inflation while expanding the company's profit margins.

These dynamics give the company solid operating leverage, pointing to healthy FFO growth ahead.

Q2 Results

In the second quarter of 2025 the company delivered results that exceeded the consensus expectations, reporting a Normalized FFO of $0.37 per share, higher than $0.35 in the first quarter, and Normalized AFFO of $0.38 per share, higher than $0.37, with an EPS of $0.27, surpassing the market estimate of $0.18 by almost $0.10.

Projections expect a Normalized FFO of $1.45 to $1.47 per share, a Normalized AFFO of $1.49 to $1.51 per share, an FFO between $1.52 and $1.54, and an AFFO between $1.47 and $1.49 per share. Meeting these projections depends on the SHOP segment, since it is more volatile and exposed to operating cost pressures paid by the company, unlike the NNN portfolio (stable and predictable), where Sabra receives fixed monthly rent checks from tenants without bearing costs. For this reason, any sudden increase in inflationary costs or high vacancy rates would represent a risk.

Sector

The Health Care REIT sector is shaped by demographic trends, with population aging being the main driver of long-term growth and demand, as the main target for senior housing and SNF services is projected to grow 56% by 2034. People in this age range with comorbidities need to be placed in supervised environments, and Sabra’s concentration in SNFs positions it to capture this demand, which is more resilient to economic conditions, and the expansion of its SHOP portfolio also allows it to capture most of the value generated by these services.

Profitability for operators in the SNF segment depends heavily on government reimbursement policies, since part of their revenue comes from public programs. Although Sabra highlights an increase in reimbursement rates of 3.2% for Medicare and 3.5% for Medicaid this year, there is room for skepticism, as these programs are the main government payment sources for hospitals and clinics, however, the rapid rise in operating costs may compress operators margins, pressuring their rent payment capacity in the future.

Valuation

FFO

SBRA's normalized P/FFO is currently being traded at around 12.5x, a slight discount compared to other healthcare REITs (14x - 34x), most likely reflecting its exposure to the SNF sector, which is seen as having a lower growth potential. In a declining interest rate environment, being in a defensive sector (REITs), the company could benefit doubly from its already high dividend yield combined with lower interest rates (higher FFO and a more attractive yield).

Using the midpoint of the 2025 Normalized FFO guidance of $1.46, a conservative metric compared to FFO, along with 2026 additions, the projected FFO per share is $1.55. A $0.05 increase in FFO is expected from the expansion of its SHOP portfolio through acquisitions. In Q2 2025, the company acquired a senior housing facility for $53 million with an estimated yield of 7.5%, in addition to securing $220 million in additional investments. The organic increase of $0.04 reflects performance in line with the company’s history, with NNN being stable and fixed-income providing a modest growth base, while SHOP, operating in a more intensive model, acts as a growth engine that captures the upside from market cycles, as the senior housing sector is experiencing historically high demand.

Dividend Yield

The company’s Normalized FFO is projected at $1.55 per share, higher than in 2025, with a dividend of $1.20 per share, consistent with its 4-year history following a dividend cut in Q2 2020, when it was $0.45 per quarter. It is important to highlight the discrepancy between its Payout Ratio, currently 155.77% (with historical levels above 200%), and its FFO Payout Ratio, currently 79.57%, historically averaging 80%. This discrepancy derives from the requirement that companies depreciate their buildings over time, reducing their net income. The probability of another cut like that is low, with only room for an increase in line with FFO growth, as analysts estimate FFO per share of $1.56, and with this value, if the dividend remains at $1.20, the Payout Ratio would improve to 76.9%, increasing dividend sustainability.

SBRA has a dividend policy of $1.20 per year, representing a dividend yield of 6.63% of the current price of $18.10. Assuming that the normalized FFO multiple remains around 12.4x, the implied target price per share for 2026 would be $19.22, an upside of 6.19%.

Financial

As a result of its financial performance, SBRA has a solid balance sheet with leverage at 5x, and it's decreasing QoQ, indicating that its profitability is surpassing its debt. Also, it had its rating upgraded to investment grade by Moody’s, demonstrating the improvement in its financial health.

The company also has more flexibility to execute its acquisition pipeline without depending on market conditions due to its liquidity profile in cash and revolving credit line, currently around $1.2 billion.

As a complement to these positive points, the strong health of its tenants supports its revenue, with rent coverage of 2.27x for the SNF portfolio. Its dividend is also well covered, indicating that its cash flow is more than sufficient to cover shareholder payments, with a Payout Ratio based on AFFO around 80%.

Drivers and Risks

The most imminent risk for SBRA is directly linked to its tenants ability to pay rent, considering that Signature Healthcare, one of its main income sources, announced ongoing financial challenges, citing dependence on government payers that are not keeping up with rising costs. In Q2 of this year, Signature represented 8.1% of Sabra’s NOI. This issue could weaken the company's finances, directly affecting its operator system, as there is a limited capacity for replacements in specialized sectors like healthcare, creating prolonged vacancy risks or the need for renegotiations under less favorable conditions. The senior population growth can also represent a risk, as it could pressure payment systems, as most SNF residents rely on Medicare and Medicaid for their medical stays and care, and with this massive increase in usage, state and federal budgets may come under tension, potentially leading to lower reimbursements. A financial crisis in these services or a recession directly affecting household savings would impact Sabra, given that its financial health is tied to that of its payers (both families and governments).

Expanding its SHOP portfolio could drive growth by increasing FFO and NOI, as the model captures revenue per occupied room and allows better control of operating costs beyond fixed rent. This transition also acts as a defense mechanism against tenant loss risk, as SHOP income comes from multiple individual residences, meaning the departure of ten or more residents would have a much smaller impact than losses from Signature, for example, which has a far greater weight. The larger the SHOP portfolio becomes, the more diluted the risk from tenant exits or cuts in the NNN segment.

Conclusion

The growth potential of its FFO, along with the expansion of its SHOP portfolio focused on capitalizing on favorable demographic trends, forms an optimistic combination, even considering its concentration in the SNF segment and the past dividend cut. For these reasons, I recommend buying Sabra’s shares, with a Target Price of $19.22.

data.title: Golar LNG: Very Attractive Adj. Ebitda Growth Outlook
data.description:
Investment Summary

I am initiating a buy rating on Golar LNG (NASDAQ:GLNG). This is an attractive company as the only proven independent operator in the FLNG market. Its liquefaction-as-a-service model directly addresses one of the energy sector’s biggest inefficiencies: the monetization of stranded gas. With two operational units and a third under construction, GLNG is transforming itself from a cyclical shipping player into a high-margin infrastructure business with multi-decade revenue visibility.

Business Overview

GLNG is a pure-play operator in the floating LNG [FLNG] space, wherein it is now focused on high-margin liquefaction platforms. Its “liquefaction-as-a-service” model delivers a fast, scalable, and capital-efficient solution for resource owners to monetize stranded gas assets. Currently, GLNG has two FLNG units already in operation and a third advanced vessel under construction.

Solid Position In A High-Barrier Market

GLNG has a strong position in the market it competes in. It is the only independent and fully proven provider of FLNG solutions (per management), a position that I think gives it both scarcity value and strong pricing power. While big energy players like Shell or Exxon have deployed FLNG assets for their own upstream use, none offer their technology and expertise as a third-party service. This is where GLNG comes in to offer a “liquefaction-as-a-service” model that enables countries and producers to monetize stranded or underutilized gas reserves without the massive capital and technical expertise typically required.

Imagine a nation or energy producer discovering a massive gas field located hundreds of kilometers offshore, with no pipelines or infrastructure to bring it to market. Traditionally, the only solution would be to build an onshore liquefaction plant (this costs a bomb, typically a multibillion-dollar, multi-year project). Few operators have the capital, engineering expertise, or appetite for that scale of investment. This is where GLNG comes in. Instead of building a permanent facility, GLNG deploys its FLNG vessel directly to the source. The vessel handles the full process, like taking in raw natural gas, purifying and cooling it to -162°C, and transferring the resulting LNG onto carriers for export. For the resource owner, this model eliminates the need to construct, own, or operate a liquefaction facility. They simply pay GLNG a predictable, long-term fee for its service.

The implication is immense, as stranded reserves that might have remained undeveloped for decades can now be monetized quickly, efficiently, and with minimal upfront cost. As such, I believe this is a critical differentiator, one that effectively makes GLNG the partner of choice for resource owners seeking to commercialize gas quickly and cost-effectively.

The execution burden and risk don’t go away but are simply transferred to GLNG, and hence having a strong track record is important. GLNG checks the box. The flawless operational record of the FLNG Hilli in Cameroon proved the commercial viability of GLNG’s FLNG technology. Another example is the FLNG Gimi, which began commercial operations under a 20-year contract with BP. This achievement has not only validated GLNG's engineering and operational capabilities, but I believe it has also helped to unlock its next growth phase. And we can see this playing out, as it was the credibility earned from Gimi that helped GLNG to win two additional 20-year FLNG contracts in Argentina through the BP-backed SESA consortium (this was mentioned by management in the Q2 call).

I see this sort of as a self-reinforcing dynamic where GLNG's proven track record continuously attracts top-tier clients, allowing it to secure long-duration contracts, which in turn gives it more credibility to win more deals.

A Fortress Balance Sheet Built On A $17 Billion Backlog

Following the wins in Argentina, GLNG now has a contracted adj. EBITDA backlog of ~$17 billion, which is massive if we consider adj. EBITDA in FY24 is only just $240 million. Also, to be clear, this backlog is not speculative pipeline opportunities but legally binding, 20-year take-or-pay charters for its core fleet (the FLNG Gimi with BP and both the FLNG Hilli and MKII FLNG under the SESA consortium).

This visibility and level of contracted revenue mean that GLNG is no longer a cyclical shipping operator. It should be seen like an infrastructure-like utility with stable, annuity-style earnings largely shielded from commodity price volatility. And this is not the end of it. By 2028, when all three vessels are fully operational, management expects run-rate adj. EBITDA of ~$800 million per year, which is more than 3x FY24 performance.

For investors, I believe this visibility is extremely valuable. It removes much of the uncertainty typically associated with energy companies and provides a direct line of sight to sustained cash flows.

A Highly Attractive Call Option On Global LNG Prices

GLNG does have some exposure to global LNG prices. Its Argentina charters have a particularly powerful kicker, one that I view as a free 20-year call option on global LNG prices. The mechanism is simple. For LNG prices above a fixed $8.00/MMBtu threshold, GLNG receives 25% of the incremental revenue, which means it basically has a share in the market upside while retaining the stability of its base charter income.

If we combine this profit-sharing mechanism from both the Hilli and Fuji contracts with its 10% equity stake in the SESA marketing entity, each $1 increase in LNG prices above $8.00/MMBtu adds roughly $100 million in incremental annual EBITDA (this is management’s math). The best thing is there is no ceiling to this upside, which means GLNG has significant operating leverage if the energy market goes into an upcycle. Just as important, the downside risk is limited. If LNG prices dip below the strike, GLNG exposure is limited to a modest tariff reduction of about $28 million, which does not really impact the cash flow of its base business.

Valuation

The way I value this is to just think of how much adj. EBITDA GLNG can produce in a steady year, which is $800 million (by 2028), and work backwards to figure out the CAGR vs. FY24—which is about 35% CAGR. This is way higher than other energy players like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which has a ~high-single-digit EBITDA CAGR outlook. With about 3x the growth rate, I think it makes sense for GLNG to trade at 21x (EPD is at 10x).

Inputting all these assumptions would get us to a share price of ~$38, which implies huge upside from where GLNG trades today.

Risk

GLNG earnings are contingent upon meeting its contracted capacity commitments, which for its upcoming projects require providing ~90% of the vessel's nameplate capacity. Any failure to achieve this guaranteed operational uptime could directly weigh on guided project returns and future EBITDA growth. Additionally, GLNG is exposed to rising operating costs. Unforeseen increases in expenses such as crew wages, insurance, or repairs and maintenance due to inflation or trade policy could compress margins and reduce profitability.

Conclusion

In my view, GLNG offers strong cash flow visibility of a long-term infrastructure utility and the upside potential of a commodity producer. The $17 billion contracted backlog, the $800 million run-rate adj. EBITDA target, and the LNG price-linked call option form a very interesting setup. I believe GLNG is materially undervalued, and therefore, I give a buy rating.

data.title: Japan Gives Traders The Head Spins
data.description:
Japan jumped into an already frothy geopolitical moment last weekend when the Liberal Democratic Party, the country’s ruling party for all but a very brief time since the end of the Second World War, selected Sanae Takaichi as the new head of the party and thus the person likely to succeed outgoing prime minister Shigeru Ishiba. Takaichi, who would thus become Japan’s first female prime minister, is known as a hardline conservative with an economic agenda modeled on the pro-stimulus policies of Shinzo Abe, the former prime minister whose “Abenomics” plan was all the rage back in the mid-2010s.

Deer in the Headlights

The news of Takaichi’s appointment caught markets flat-footed. The Japanese yen has been up against the dollar for most of this year, while the Nikkei 225 stock index mostly meandered along with modest gains. But the prospect of intense political pressure on the Bank of Japan to lower interest rates and pump up the economy had an immediate effect on both stocks (sending them soaring) and the yen (which promptly nosedived on the news).

On Monday morning, traders were quick to dub the weekend’s developments as the “Takaichi trade,” with the Nikkei index ending the day nearly five percent higher. Observers noted the particular attractiveness of shares in the defense, energy and cybersecurity sectors given Takaichi’s hawkish bent on strengthening Japan’s military capabilities. Meanwhile, a bond market expecting to see another interest rate hike when the Bank of Japan meets in a couple weeks had a deer-in-the-headlights moment, with Takaichi vowing in public statements to put pressure on the BoJ to keep rates low in service of new rounds of economic stimulus.

Coalition Problems

But the market gyrations were far from over as the week wore on. The fortunes of the Liberal Democratic Party have been poor of late, with the party losing its majority in both the upper and lower houses of parliament during the troubled rule of Ishida, the outgoing PM. The party has been able to continue governing thanks to its coalition with the Komeito party. But that came apart earlier today when Komeito’s leader, Tetsuo Saito, pulled the junior party out of the coalition. Lacking an outright majority, Takaishi will now have to obtain support from other minority parties in order to form a governing coalition. That could potentially pull Japan even further to the right, joining what appears to be a global trend gathering steam.

Despite the Komeito setback, Takaichi is likely to be sworn in as prime minister following parliamentary voting next week. Traders with itchy fingers may want to wait for a while to see what the make-up of the new governing coalition looks like, though, before diving head-first into the “Takaichi trade.” Japan is in a different place today than it was in 2012 when then-PM Abe kicked off his “Abenomics” agenda. The new prime minister and her government are going to have to adapt their policies to address concerns about higher consumer prices, a rapidly ageing demographic landscape, and the regional challenges facing all countries residing in the neighborhood dominated by China. We will be paying close attention to developments in Japan as we proceed with our own geographic diversification strategy. But for now, our take on Japan is very much wait and see.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
